rootCauseAnalysis.summary: The demo-diskpressure namespace contains multiple workloads deliberately designed to exhaust node ephemeral storage via large emptyDir writes, causing repeated DiskPressure eviction cycles on dev-worker-1.redhat-internal.com. The primary contributor is postgres-emptydir, which uses an 8Gi emptyDir volume for its PostgreSQL data directory — the single largest allocation. Combined emptyDir limits (~10 GiB) across all workloads exceed the node's allocatable ephemeral storage (~7.9 GiB), triggering a rolling eviction cycle every 25-35 minutes. The node is GitOps-managed via ArgoCD, making a GitOps-driven migration of the emptyDir to a PVC the correct remediation path.
causalChain: Signal: DiskPressure predicted (and confirmed) on node dev-worker-1.redhat-internal.com
Why? Kubelet fired EvictionThresholdMet for ephemeral-storage at 13:20:21 UTC, entering DiskPressure condition at 13:20:28 UTC
Why? Multiple pods in demo-diskpressure namespace are actively writing large volumes of data to emptyDir volumes backed by node ephemeral storage

Why? Combined emptyDir allocations (~10 GiB across 4 Deployments) exceed the node's 7.9 GiB allocatable ephemeral storage, and the rolling eviction cycle causes continuous re-writes as evicted pods are rescheduled

Root cause: The demo-diskpressure namespace Deployments (especially postgres-emptydir with 8Gi emptyDir) are deliberately configured to saturate node ephemeral storage with no persistent storage backend, causing a self-reinforcing eviction-reschedule-rewrite cycle

dueDiligence.targetAccuracy: Targeting postgres-emptydir Deployment because its 8Gi emptyDir is the single largest contributor to ephemeral storage exhaustion. Migrating this to a PVC is the most targeted fix. Other contributing deployments (nginx-cache, log-collector, redis-scratch) are listed in contributing_factors. The node itself is the signal reporter, not the fix target.
dueDiligence.proportionality: Remediation target (postgres-emptydir) is the most specifically remediable resource — its 8Gi emptyDir can be replaced with a PVC in a well-defined workflow. The other deployments are secondary contributors and listed in contributing_factors. Scaling down the entire namespace is also valid if this is a completed test.
dueDiligence.scopeCompleteness: Investigated: node describe + events, all 5 pods in demo-diskpressure namespace, all 4 Deployments (log-collector, nginx-cache, postgres-emptydir, redis-scratch), Prometheus filesystem metrics (range + instant), container_fs_usage_bytes per pod, node_filesystem_avail/size_bytes. Did not investigate: kubelet configuration for eviction thresholds (not directly accessible), LVMS/TopoLVM volume groups (not relevant to emptyDir pressure).
dueDiligence.causalCompleteness: Traced from signal → eviction event → emptyDir writes → combined allocation exceeding node capacity → specific deployment configurations. Cannot go deeper as the workloads are intentionally designed for disk pressure simulation. The postgres-emptydir 8Gi emptyDir is the deepest specific configuration cause.
dueDiligence.evidenceSufficiency: All claims backed by: kubectl_describe (node conditions, deployment specs), kubectl_events (EvictionThresholdMet at 13:20:21, NodeHasDiskPressure at 13:20:28), Prometheus range queries (pod eviction cycle over 110 minutes, /var filesystem at 72.6% and trending up), node_filesystem_avail/size_bytes (17.5 GiB free on /dev/vda4). Deployment command specs confirm exact write patterns. No inaccessible data sources.
dueDiligence.regressionAwareness: No prior remediation history available for this node or the demo-diskpressure namespace workloads per get_cluster_resource_context output.
dueDiligence.alternativeHypotheses: Considered: (1) Node-level OS disk fill from non-pod sources — ruled out: /var at 72.6% with 17.5 GiB free; pressure is specifically on ephemeral-storage accounting layer. (2) Container image layer accumulation — possible minor contributor but not the primary driver given the explicit write commands in pod specs. (3) Single workload responsible — ruled out: multiple workloads all contribute, but postgres-emptydir's 8Gi limit is the dominant factor. (4) Transient/one-time event — ruled out: 4 successive eviction cycles observed over 110 minutes confirms persistent, recurring pressure.
dueDiligence.confidenceCalibration: 0.93: Starting at 1.0. -0.04 for not being able to directly inspect kubelet eviction threshold configuration (to confirm exact threshold values). -0.03 for the fact that the emptyDir sizeLimit is a soft limit enforced by kubelet, and actual disk consumption may differ from the declared limits. The core causal chain is fully evidenced.

contributingFactors: postgres-emptydir uses an 8Gi emptyDir for PostgreSQL data directory — the largest single ephemeral storage contributor
nginx-cache init container writes ~200MB of random data on every pod start to emptyDir (512Mi limit)
log-collector writes a new 32K file every 60 seconds indefinitely (up to 512Mi emptyDir)
redis-scratch session-loader sidecar writes ~100MB at startup per pod (×2 pods)
Combined emptyDir limits (~10 GiB) exceed the node's allocatable ephemeral storage (~7.9 GiB)
Node /var filesystem at 72.6% used and trending upward
Rolling eviction cycle: 4 successive pod generations evicted and rescheduled every 25-35 minutes
NoSchedule taint applied at 13:32:06 UTC prevents new scheduling but does not stop existing disk-writing pods

kaResponse.analysis: The demo-diskpressure namespace contains multiple workloads deliberately designed to exhaust node ephemeral storage via large emptyDir writes, causing repeated DiskPressure eviction cycles on dev-worker-1.redhat-internal.com. The primary contributor is postgres-emptydir, which uses an 8Gi emptyDir volume for its PostgreSQL data directory — the single largest allocation. Combined emptyDir limits (~10 GiB) across all workloads exceed the node's allocatable ephemeral storage (~7.9 GiB), triggering a rolling eviction cycle every 25-35 minutes. The node is GitOps-managed via ArgoCD, making a GitOps-driven migration of the emptyDir to a PVC the correct remediation path.
